zones.name: LLM Provider Core
zones.description: The central abstraction layer defining provider interfaces, client factory, token tracking, and adapters for Claude API, CLI, and Codex backends.
insights: 12 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
High cohesion (0.77) confirms that provider interface, client creation, and type definitions are tightly and correctly coupled together.
This zone is the dependency target for both the utility layer (23 imports) and the test suite (25 imports), confirming its role as the true public API surface.
The 3 reverse imports back into src-2 introduce a bidirectional dependency that should be examined — src-level abstractions should not depend on runtime utilities.
Cohesion of 0.77 reflects a well-structured core module where providers, types, and factory logic are appropriately co-located.

3 imports from src into src-2 create a bidirectional dependency with the utility layer; if those utilities are truly generic, consider moving the depended-upon items into src or a shared foundation file.
Multiple named entry points (api-provider, cli-provider, create-client, etc.) indicate a deliberate multi-surface export strategy aligned with the package's vendor-neutral design goal.

llm-provider-core is the hub in a hub-and-spoke topology: both llm-runtime-support (23 imports) and llm-provider-tests (25 imports) depend on it, but neither depends on each other — core is the sole integration point.

The 3 downward imports from core into support suggest utilities were logically promoted to core status but physically left in support — the fix is to either move those 3 utilities up into src/ or extract them into a shared foundation file rather than reaching down.

llm-provider-core acts as a hub with two independent spoke zones (support and tests); this is a sound layered pattern as long as the hub does not import from its spokes — the 3 reverse imports into support break this invariant.

The interface between llm-provider-core and llm-provider-tests is clean and one-directional (25 test imports, 0 reverse) — this is a well-defined boundary and should be preserved as a model for the support layer.

config.ts is the sole origin of all 3 bidirectional edges: api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, and create-client.ts each import only from config.ts in the support zone — moving config.ts to the core zone would eliminate the cycle in a single file move with no other changes required.

create-client.ts calls resolveApiKey from config.ts directly rather than accepting a resolved key as a parameter — the factory hard-codes a config resolution side-effect, making unit testing without filesystem fixtures impossible and coupling the factory to the config layer.

codex-cli-provider.ts resolves configuration inline (resolveCodexModel, codexConfig parameter) without using the shared config.ts utilities, while cli-provider.ts delegates to resolveCliPath — two CLI providers use divergent config resolution strategies within the same zone.

create-client.ts imports resolveApiKey from config.ts (support zone) rather than accepting a pre-resolved key — factory functions should receive resolved values via parameters, not perform I/O side-effects internally. This couples the factory to the filesystem and makes isolated unit testing require real config fixtures.

codex-cli-provider.ts and cli-provider.ts implement divergent config resolution strategies: cli-provider.ts delegates to resolveCliPath from config.ts while codex-cli-provider.ts resolves config inline via a passed codexConfig parameter. The inconsistency means the two CLI provider implementations cannot be substituted interchangeably and will diverge further as config logic evolves.

createLLMClient() in llm-client.ts returns ClaudeClient rather than LLMProvider — the top-level vendor-neutral factory bypasses the generic provider interface it was designed to unify, so callers typed to LLMProvider cannot use createLLMClient() without a cast.

Duplicate vendor dispatch exists across provider-registry.ts and llm-client.ts: both contain independent if-else branches on the 'codex' vendor name that must be kept in sync when new vendors are added. provider-registry.ts is the designated extensibility point, but llm-client.ts maintains a parallel hard-coded dispatch that circumvents the registry.

suggest.ts and help-format.ts export pure CLI presentation utilities (Levenshtein distance, color formatting) with no imports from any provider or LLM-related module, yet they are first-class exports of the foundation package. They are in the core zone by file placement but have no conceptual relationship to LLM provider infrastructure.

createLLMClient() return type is ClaudeClient instead of LLMProvider — change the return type annotation so the vendor-neutral factory returns the generic interface. createClient() already returns ClaudeClient & LLMProvider, so only the llm-client.ts wrapper needs updating.

llm-client.ts hardcodes a codex branch independently of ProviderRegistry — callers adding a third vendor must update both files. Remove the manual dispatch from llm-client.ts and delegate to defaultRegistry.getActiveProvider() to make the registry the single extension point.

suggest.ts and help-format.ts are CLI presentation utilities with no LLM logic — their presence in the public.ts barrel inflates the package's surface with non-LLM concerns. Consider grouping them under a documented 'CLI utilities' section in public.ts or extracting to a subpath export (e.g. @n-dx/llm-client/cli) to signal the different role.
[call graph] 55 internal calls, 4 outgoing, 88 incoming (cohesion: 0.93, coupling: 0.07)

zones.name: LLM Provider Tests
zones.description: Unit test suite with one-to-one coverage of every core provider, client, registry, session, and type module in llm-provider-core.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm the test zone is fully self-contained and correctly structured as a leaf in the dependency graph.
25 imports from tests into src-core with zero reverse imports is the ideal test-to-source relationship — no production code depends on tests.

One-to-one file correspondence between tests and source modules (9 test files for 12+ source files) suggests slight gaps; provider-session and token-usage have tests, but codex-cli-provider and llm-config may not.
Perfect cohesion and zero coupling are the gold standard for a test zone — this suite is cleanly isolated from all non-test concerns.
codex-cli-provider.ts and llm-config.ts do not appear to have corresponding test files; verify coverage parity across all provider implementations.
All 25 test-to-source imports target llm-provider-core exclusively, confirming the utility layer (src-2) currently has no dedicated unit tests.

The test zone's exclusive targeting of llm-provider-core (25 imports, 0 to support) creates an implicit architectural split: core is 'tested and stable', support is 'untested and volatile' — this asymmetry increases risk for the support zone during refactoring.

Test coverage is structurally biased toward llm-provider-core; no test files import from llm-runtime-support, creating a coverage boundary that coincides with the zone boundary — adding a tests/unit/support/ directory would close this gap and make the zone boundary explicit in the test layout.

codex-cli-provider.ts has no corresponding test file in the test zone despite being one of the two concrete provider implementations — the gap is asymmetric since api-provider.ts and cli-provider.ts both have tests, leaving the codex provider as the only untested provider.

codex-cli-provider.ts is the only concrete provider implementation without a dedicated test file; api-provider.test.ts and cli-provider.test.ts exist but codex-cli-provider.test.ts does not. Since codex-cli-provider.ts uses raw spawn and inline config resolution (diverging from the shared utilities used by other providers), it has the highest behavioral complexity and the lowest test safety net.

The codex provider registered in createDefaultRegistry() explicitly declares capabilities: [] and omits validateAuth(), while the comment acknowledges it as a temporary wrapper pending a real LLMProvider implementation — but no corresponding test exercises the codex registry path. The absence of any test for the defaultRegistry codex branch means the documented tech-debt shim has no regression guard.

No test exercises the defaultRegistry codex factory path or verifies that createDefaultRegistry() returns a valid LLMProvider for the codex vendor — add a test to provider-registry.test.ts that calls createDefaultRegistry().create('codex', {...}) and asserts info.vendor === 'codex' to create a regression guard around the documented temporary wrapper.
[call graph] 78 internal calls, 88 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 0.47, coupling: 0.53)

zones.name: LLM Runtime Support
zones.description: Cross-cutting utilities and configuration helpers — auth, exec, JSON handling, output formatting, project config, and directory resolution — that underpin the provider implementations.
insights: Contains 43% of project files (19/44) — may be too broad, consider splitting
Moderate cohesion (0.56) suggests this zone is a collection of loosely related utilities rather than a single coherent concern; consider whether auth, exec, and output belong in separate modules.
With 23 imports into llm-provider-core, this zone acts as a dependency on core types/interfaces, which is the correct direction for a support layer.
The single entry point (src/config.ts) may be underdeclaring the zone's public surface; utilities like auth.ts and exec.ts are likely consumed directly by providers.
Cohesion of 0.56 is below the strong-cohesion threshold, indicating this zone bundles heterogeneous utilities; splitting auth/exec/output into focused sub-modules would improve navigability.
11 additional files beyond the listed 8 suggest significant breadth — audit whether all utilities are actively consumed or whether dead code has accumulated here.
project-config.ts and project-dirs.ts serving as 'types' alongside runtime utilities like exec.ts indicates mixed abstraction levels within this zone.

The support zone has no test coverage visible in the import graph (tests/unit/ imports exclusively target llm-provider-core), meaning the 19-file support zone is entirely untested — this is the largest test coverage gap in the package.

The support zone's role as a dependency of core (23 imports upward) while also receiving 3 imports back from core means it simultaneously acts as a foundation layer and a utility layer — these two roles should be separated to resolve the bidirectional coupling.

llm-runtime-support is entirely absent from the test import graph — all 25 test-to-source imports target llm-provider-core exclusively, leaving the largest zone in the package with zero unit test coverage.

The support zone plays two conflicting roles: foundation (23 imports into core treat it as a lower layer) and utility consumer (3 reverse imports from core treat it as a higher layer) — this dual-role is the root cause of the bidirectional coupling and should drive the refactor boundary.

exec.ts explicitly documents in JSDoc that streaming consumers should use spawn directly from node:child_process — the two CLI providers bypassing exec.ts for their spawn calls is by design, not an oversight. exec.ts is correctly scoped to non-streaming subprocess orchestration.

auth.ts is the only file in the support zone that orchestrates multiple other support utilities (config.ts + exec.ts) rather than providing a standalone utility — it operates at a higher abstraction level than the other files in this zone, suggesting it is a provider-level concern misplaced in the utility layer.

auth.ts coordinates config resolution (resolveApiKey, resolveCliPath) and process execution (exec) to authenticate providers — this is provider-level orchestration logic, not a standalone utility. Placing it in the support zone alongside pure utilities (json.ts, output.ts, project-dirs.ts) inflates the zone's apparent cohesion score and obscures the architectural role of authentication.

config.ts is imported by 3 files in the core zone (api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, create-client.ts) and 1 file in the support zone (auth.ts) — it serves the core zone more than the support zone. Its physical placement in support is the sole cause of the bidirectional coupling; relocating it to the core zone would reduce the architectural risk to zero without changing any logic.

AuthMode (types.ts) and ProviderAuthMode (provider-interface.ts) are parallel auth-mode types serving the same semantic role but with different extensibility: AuthMode is a closed union ('api' | 'cli') while ProviderAuthMode is open-ended. ClaudeClient.mode is typed to AuthMode and LLMProvider.info.mode to ProviderAuthMode, preventing structural compatibility between the two interfaces without a cast even when the value is identical.

codex-cli-provider.ts hardcodes DEFAULT_CODEX_MODEL = 'gpt-5-codex' directly in the provider file with no counterpart in config.ts or llm-config.ts where Claude default model constants live — the two vendors follow divergent conventions for default model resolution.

The OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable stripping in codex-cli-provider.ts is an undocumented external dependency: if OPENAI_API_KEY is present in the environment, it overrides Codex CLI session credentials, causing auth failures that are silently retried with the key removed. This environmental assumption is not documented in config.ts or llm-config.ts where other env-var conventions are described.

AuthMode (types.ts) and ProviderAuthMode (provider-interface.ts) are duplicate auth-mode types — AuthMode should be replaced with ProviderAuthMode or aliased to it so ClaudeClient.mode and ProviderInfo.mode share the same type. The current mismatch makes ClaudeClient structurally incompatible with LLMProvider even for equivalent values.

codex-cli-provider.ts hardcodes DEFAULT_CODEX_MODEL without a config.ts counterpart — add a resolveCodexModel utility to config.ts parallel to resolveModel() for Claude to establish a single authoritative default-model resolution path per vendor.

The OPENAI_API_KEY retry-without-key workaround in codex-cli-provider.ts embeds an undocumented environmental assumption about Codex CLI credential precedence — document this behavior in a JSDoc block or inline comment at the point where CodexConfig is defined in llm-types.ts so downstream consumers know the env interaction exists.
[call graph] 269 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 4 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Package Config
zones.description: Project-level configuration files that define build, type-checking, and test runner settings for the llm-client package.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 3 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
These three files form a self-contained configuration layer with no internal imports, correctly isolated from source code.
vitest.config.ts ties test execution to the package boundary, supporting the monorepo's per-package test strategy.
No entry points here is expected — config files are consumed by tooling, not by application code.
Zero cohesion and zero coupling scores are expected for a config-only zone; this is correct and healthy.
All three files serve distinct tooling concerns (npm metadata, TypeScript, Vitest) with no overlap — well-separated responsibilities.
Zero import edges in or out of this zone confirms it acts as a true configuration boundary — tooling consumes these files externally, so the absence of internal import edges is by design and healthy.
package-config has no import relationships with any other zone — it is the only zone in this package that is fully decoupled at the import graph level, making it a clean configuration boundary.
Bidirectional coupling: "llm-provider-core" ↔ "llm-runtime-support" (3+23 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 19 files vs smallest with 3 — uneven decomposition

The bidirectional dependency between llm-provider-core (src) and llm-runtime-support (src-2) is the primary architectural risk — 23 imports flow up from support to core while 3 flow back down; resolve the downward 3 to eliminate the cycle.
The llm-runtime-support zone has no test coverage visible in this graph; adding a tests/unit/support/ directory would close the gap.

The overall dependency flow (config → core ← support ← tests) is sound and matches a clean layered architecture; the only correction needed is severing the 3 reverse imports from core back into support.
Three of four zones have no circular imports; the one bidirectional edge (src ↔ src-2) is the only structural issue in an otherwise well-ordered package.
The llm-runtime-support zone's cohesion (0.56) is the weakest signal in the package — decomposing it into auth, exec, and config sub-zones would bring every zone above 0.7.

The package correctly concentrates all external-facing provider abstractions in a single high-cohesion core zone, which aligns with the llm-client's stated vendor-neutral foundation role in the monorepo.
tests/unit/ contains files from 3 zones (llm-provider-core, llm-provider-tests, llm-runtime-support) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries
File "tests/unit/api-provider.test.ts" has 100% of import neighbors in src/ but lives in tests/unit/ — consider relocating

The overall import graph follows a hub-and-spoke pattern with llm-provider-core at the center — this is a valid and common architecture, but the 3 reverse imports from core into support are the single deviation that converts a clean layered graph into one with a cycle.

The only zone pair with a well-defined, one-directional interface is llm-provider-core ↔ llm-provider-tests (25 imports, 0 reverse) — this pair should be the model for how the core ↔ support boundary should be restructured.

package-config is fully decoupled from all runtime zones, creating a clean two-cluster structure: one config cluster (package-config) and one runtime cluster (core + support + tests) with no cross-cluster imports.

The package has a hub-and-spoke topology with llm-provider-core as hub; the only structural defect is 3 edges flowing from hub back to spoke (src → src-2), which should be eliminated to restore a strict DAG.
The absence of any import path from llm-runtime-support to llm-provider-tests confirms no test utilities leak into production code — the test isolation boundary is intact across all zone pairs.

Test coverage follows zone boundaries exactly — tests cover the hub (core) but not the spoke (support); this means the zone with the most files and lowest cohesion (support, 19 files, 0.56) has the least test safety net during any refactor.

The 3 bidirectional edges between llm-provider-core and llm-runtime-support all originate from a single file (config.ts) being consumed by core providers — this is a file placement problem, not a design problem. A single file move resolves the only structural defect in the package without any logic changes.

public.ts (the barrel re-export file) imports from both config.ts and exec.ts in the support zone in addition to core-zone files — its cross-zone imports may not be fully reflected in the reported '3 imports' edge count, meaning the actual bidirectional coupling surface could be wider than the zone graph currently shows.

The two CLI providers (cli-provider.ts and codex-cli-provider.ts) implement incompatible config resolution contracts: one depends on shared config utilities (config.ts), the other accepts config as a parameter. As the package adds more vendors this inconsistency will compound — a unified provider options interface that resolves config before construction would prevent further divergence.

The package has two parallel but incompatible patterns for providing configuration to providers: implicit resolution via config.ts utilities (used by api-provider, cli-provider, create-client) vs explicit parameter injection (used by codex-cli-provider). New vendor implementations will have no canonical pattern to follow, leading to further inconsistency as the provider registry grows.

CompletionRequest.cliFlags (types.ts) is a transport-specific field in the vendor-neutral request contract — API providers must silently ignore it while CLI providers depend on it, coupling the shared interface to the CLI transport. A cleaner boundary would accept provider-specific request extensions via an opaque 'providerOptions' bag or separate the CLI-specific fields into CliCompletionRequest extends CompletionRequest.

The package exports two parallel client creation paths — createClient()/detectAuthMode() for Claude and createLLMClient()/detectLLMAuthMode() for multi-vendor — with nearly identical option bags and detection logic. This doubles the API surface without adding capability; consolidating to a single createLLMClient() path that delegates to the registry for all vendors would eliminate the redundancy.

LLMClient = ClaudeClient (llm-types.ts) is described as a migration shim but has no deprecation annotation or issue reference pointing to when it will be removed — adding a @deprecated JSDoc tag with a migration note would make the intended lifecycle explicit and prevent new callers from relying on the alias.

CompletionRequest.cliFlags leaks a CLI-only concern into the vendor-neutral request type used by LLMProvider — rename it to providerFlags or move it to a CliCompletionRequest subtype so API providers do not receive transport-specific fields they must silently discard.

Two parallel factory entry points (createClient / createLLMClient) with overlapping option shapes increase the API surface without differentiating capability — designate createLLMClient() as the sole public factory and deprecate createClient() as an internal implementation detail to reduce consumer confusion.

LLMClient = ClaudeClient has no @deprecated annotation or timeline for removal despite being described as a migration shim — add a JSDoc @deprecated tag referencing the intended replacement (LLMProvider) so static analysis tools surface it at call sites and the migration debt does not silently accumulate.
[call graph] 88 calls: "llm-provider-tests" → "llm-provider-core"
